perturbation.original_text: Consultant will report as self-employment income all compensation received by Consultant pursuant to this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Consultant will report as self-employment income all compensation received, and no deductions will be made for California State Disability Insurance (SDI).
perturbation.explanation: Adding that no deductions will be made for SDI is problematic. If the consultant is misclassified as an independent contractor but should be an employee under California law, failure to deduct and remit SDI contributions violates California law.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Unemployment Insurance Code Section 906 requires employers to withhold and remit employee contributions for State Disability Insurance (SDI) if the individual is classified as an employee. By stating that no deductions will be made, even if the consultant should be classified as an employee, the contract violates this section, because it restricts proper payments to the SDI fund. If the consultant is misclassified, SDI is still required.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, UIC 906 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Unemployment Insurance Code - UIC DIVISION 1. UNEMPLOYMENT AND DISABILITY COMPENSATION [100 - 4751] ( Division 1 enacted by Stats. 1953, Ch. 308. ) PART 1. UNEMPLOYMENT COMPENSATION [100 - 2129] ( Part 1 enacted by Stats. 1953, Ch. 308. ) CHAPTER 4. Contributions and Reports [901 - 1243] ( Chapter 4 enacted by Stats. 1953, Ch. 308. ) ARTICLE 1. Definitions [901 - 906] ( Article 1 enacted by Stats. 1953, Ch. 308. ) 906. “Contributions paid on his own behalf” means: (a) All contributions paid under this part to the Unemployment Fund on behalf of an employer with respect to wages paid by him on or before the computation date for any rating period, which have been paid on or before the end of the calendar month next succeeding such computation date. (b) Any additional amount of contributions paid under this part to the Unemployment Fund on behalf of an employer with respect to wages paid by him on or before the computation date for any rating period, which have been assessed pursuant to Section 1036 and have been paid before the delinquent date for the calendar quarter in which he is given notice of the assessment pursuant to Section 1036. (c) Any additional contributions paid pursuant to Section 976.5. (Amended by Stats. 1989, Ch. 1146, Sec. 2.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text creates a legal contradiction. By stating that no deductions will be made for California State Disability Insurance (SDI), the contract violates California Unemployment Insurance Code Section 906 if the consultant should be classified as an employee. The altered language directly undermines the obligation to withhold and remit SDI contributions if the consultant is misclassified. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing UIC 906 discusses contributions paid under the part to the Unemployment Fund on behalf of an employer with respect to wages paid. The added clause explicitly restricts payments, potentially leading to non-compliance.

perturbation.original_text: Consultant will not, during the Term, and for a period of one (1) year thereafter, directly or indirectly: (i) solicit, recruit or promote the solicitation or recruitment of any employee or consultant of the Company for the purpose of encouraging that employee or consultant to leave the Company's employ or sever an agreement for services; or (ii) solicit, participate in or promote the solicitation of any of the Company's clients, customers, or prospective customers with whom Consultant had a Material Contact (hereinafter defined) and/or regarding whom Consultant received Confidential Information, for the purpose of providing products or services ("Competitive Products/Services").
perturbation.changed_text: Consultant will not, during the Term, and for a period of two (2) years thereafter, directly or indirectly: (i) solicit, recruit or promote the solicitation or recruitment of any employee or consultant of the Company for the purpose of encouraging that employee or consultant to leave the Company's employ or sever an agreement for services; or (ii) solicit, participate in or promote the solicitation of any of the Company's clients, customers, or prospective customers with whom Consultant had a Material Contact (hereinafter defined) and/or regarding whom Consultant received Confidential Information, for the purpose of providing products or services ("Competitive Products/Services").
perturbation.explanation: Extending the non-solicitation period to two years may be deemed unreasonable and unenforceable under California Business and Professions Code Section 16600, especially for independent contractors. While non-solicitation clauses are viewed differently from non-compete clauses, a two-year restriction might be considered an unlawful restraint of trade if it unduly restricts the consultant's ability to earn a living.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600 generally invalidates contracts that restrain a person from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business. While narrowly tailored non-solicitation agreements may be enforceable, a two-year period might be considered too broad and restrictive, particularly given California's strong policy against restraints of trade. A non-solicitation agreement of that length restricts the consultant's options, and a court might deem it illegal.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the non-solicitation period to two years, which the Perturbation Explanation suggests may be unreasonable and unenforceable under California Business and Professions Code Section 16600. The Law Explanation supports this by stating that a two-year period might be considered too broad and restrictive, violating California's policy against restraints of trade. Scraped Law Snippet 1 reinforces this, stating that 'every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void,' with an emphasis on broadly voiding non-compete agreements that don't satisfy specific exceptions.
